title: Dog Growth on the Eyelid Margin: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticing a bump on your dog's eyelid? Learn about meibomian tumors, when to see a vet, and why a photo is essential for triage.
content_html:
What is a Growth on the Eyelid Margin?

Finding a new bump on your dog's eyelid can be concerning. Most commonly, these are meibomian gland tumors, which are benign growths arising from the oil-producing glands of the eyelid. Other possibilities include papillomas (warts), chalazions (clogged glands), or, in rarer cases, malignant tumors. While many of these growths are slow-growing and non-cancerous, they can still cause physical irritation to the eye's surface.

Urgency Level: Low

In most cases, the urgency level for an eyelid growth is low. If your dog is acting normally and the eye itself is clear, this is typically not a medical emergency. However, you should schedule a routine veterinary appointment to have the mass evaluated. If you notice the eye becoming red, your dog squinting, or a thick discharge appearing, the urgency increases, as the growth may be scratching the cornea (the surface of the eye).

How a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, close-up photo of the growth is one of the most helpful things you can do. A high-quality image allows a veterinary professional to assess the texture, color, and exact position of the mass relative to the eyeball. Because these growths can change slowly over time, having a baseline photo helps your vet determine if the mass is growing rapidly or changing in a way that requires surgical intervention.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter human eye drops on the growth?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or eye drops on your dog without a veterinarian's approval, as some ingredients can be toxic or worsen an underlying eye condition.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery to remove the growth?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. If the growth is small and not causing irritation to the eye, your vet may choose to monitor it. However, if it grows large enough to rub against the cornea, surgical removal is often recommended to prevent painful ulcers.

faqs.question: Are eyelid growths usually cancerous?
faqs.answer: In dogs, the vast majority of eyelid margin tumors (around 75% to 90%) are benign. However, only a veterinarian can confirm the type of growth through an exam or biopsy.
